coredata.dc:title: Pyridine functionalized carbon dots for specific detection of tryptophan in human serum samples and living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenshuai
Huang, Saipeng
Wen, Huiyun
Luo, Yane
Cheng, Jiewei
Jia, Zhao
Han, Pu
Xue, Weiming
coredata.dc:description: Tryptophan (Trp) serves as an essential amino acid in protein and peptide metabolism of human body. Sensitive detection and quantification of Trp is imperative for the early diagnosis and prevention of Trp-associated disease. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective carbon quantum dot (Py-CDs) was fabricated and synthesized by a high-efficiency, one-pot hydrothermal route utilizing pyridine (Py) derivative 3-aminoisonicotinic acid. The biosensor with superior optical and biological characteristics exhibited excellent water solubility, good photostability, high biocompatibility and great cellular imaging capability. The Py-CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward Trp with low detection limits of 5.7 nM. The mechanism research of high selectivity revealed the surface pyridine ring of Py-CDs served as an active site and formed the table nonfluorescence composite with tryptophan within just 15 s. Meanwhile, the biosensor has realized tryptophan detection in human serum samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery. Furthermore, the biosensor was also extended to efficient detection of intracellular Trp, which illustrated its bright prospect and great potential in practical application of tryptophan associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tryptophan
Carbon dots
Pyridine
Biosensor
Human serum
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cellular imaging and detection of tryptophan
Fluorescence sensing of Trp based on the Py-CDs
Possible mechanism of the optical response of Py-CDs to Trp
Detection of Trp in biological samples
Cytotoxcity test and imaging of Trp in living cell systems

This study was also supported by Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project (Youth) ?2019JQ-461?.

This study was also supported by Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project (Youth) “ 2019JQ-461 ”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a), HR TEM (b) images of Py-CDs, (c) the particle size distribution (c) and FT-IR spectra (d) of Py-CDs.
Full-survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (a), C1s (b), N 1 s (c), and O1s (d) XPS spectra of Py-CDs.

(a) UV−vis spectrum, the inset is the optical graph of Py-CDs under day light and excitation with 365 nm; (b) Emission fluorescence spectra under various excitation wavelength (from 345 nm to 415 nm); (c) Time-dependent fluorescence spectra of Py-CDs quenched by 20 μM Trp; (d) Fluorescent intensity variation of the Py-CDs under uninterrupted irradiation for 60 min.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Py-CDs with various amounts of Trp (0–40 μM); (b) Stern−Volmer plot of Py-CDs quenched by Trp within the range of 0.02–20 μM, where F0 and F are fluorescent intensities of the Py-CDs in the absence and presence of Trp, respectively; (c) Fluorescent intensity response (F/F0) of Py-CDs toward various amino acids, GSH and glucose (Trp, 40 μM; Ala, Arg, Asp, Cys, Gln, His, Ser, Thr, Tyr, GSH and glucose, 200 μM; black bars) and the subsequent addition of 40 μM Trp (red bars); (d) Fluorescent intensity response (F/F0) of the Py-CDs toward various inorganic ions (Trp, 40 μM; Na +, Ca 2+, Mg 2+, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 − and NO3 −, 200 μM; black bars) and the subsequent addition of 40 μM Trp (red bars).

(a) Fluorescence decay traces of Py-CDs in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of Trp; (b) Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of the Py-CDs using the standard MTT assay toward L929 cells.
Schematic illustrating the possible sensing mechanism of Py-CDs to Trp.

Fluorescence microscopy images (a-c) and their corresponding bright-field transmission images (d-f) of L929 cells: (a,d) with 20 μg/mL Py-CDs, (b,e) with 20 μg/mL Py-CDs + 20 μM Trp and (c,f) with 20 μg/mL Py-CDs + 40 μM Trp.
Fluorescence recovery experiments of Trp from human serum samples.
Comparison of major characteristics of different fluorescent sensors of tryptophan.

head.ce:title: Pyridine functionalized carbon dots for specific detection of tryptophan in human serum samples and living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi′an,710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi′an,710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tryptophan (Trp) serves as an essential amino acid in protein and peptide metabolism of human body. Sensitive detection and quantification of Trp is imperative for the early diagnosis and prevention of Trp-associated disease. Herein, a highly sensitive and selective carbon quantum dot (Py-CDs) was fabricated and synthesized by a high-efficiency, one-pot hydrothermal route utilizing pyridine (Py) derivative 3-aminoisonicotinic acid. The biosensor with superior optical and biological characteristics exhibited excellent water solubility, good photostability, high biocompatibility and great cellular imaging capability. The Py-CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward Trp with low detection limits of 5.7 nM. The mechanism research of high selectivity revealed the surface pyridine ring of Py-CDs served as an active site and formed the table nonfluorescence composite with tryptophan within just 15 s. Meanwhile, the biosensor has realized tryptophan detection in human serum samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery. Furthermore, the biosensor was also extended to efficient detection of intracellular Trp, which illustrated its bright prospect and great potential in practical application of tryptophan associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Tryptophan (Trp), as an essential amino acid in protein and peptide metabolism in human body, plays a significant and indispensable role in human diet for establishing and maintaining nitrogen balance [1–3]. Besides, tryptophan serves as a significant role in nervous system [4]. In particular, tryptophan, which cannot be synthesized in the human body, has to be taken by foods in daily [5]. According to World Health Organization (WHO), tryptophan must be controlled about 4 mg/kg in living body [6], while the abnormal tryptophan content can cause delusional and hallucination disorders that even give rise to both psychiatric disorders and neurodegenerative diseases, such as schizophrenia and Parkinson's syndrome [7]. In addition, endogenous tryptophan in biological fluid (especially in blood serum) is a crucial signal source for hepatocellular disease diagnosis, which can be utilized as important marker for early diagnosis and prevention of liver cancer patients [6]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a simple, facile, low toxicity and high-efficiency analytical approach for tryptophan detection in various conditions especially in biological systems, which will illustrate profound significance and practical value for disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.

Owing to the vital biological functions of tryptophan, multiple methods have been built to identify and quantify tryptophan. Compared with the traditional analytical techniques such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [9], gas chromatography (GC) [10] and electrochemical techniques [11], fluorescent biosensors have been extensively applied to detect tryptophan due to their simple operation, visualization, high selectivity and sensitivity. Whereas, it is noteworthy that the detection mechanisms of most reported tryptophan fluorescent molecule sensors requires a numerous aromatic fluorophores in close proximity to create π-π stacking interactions. For instance, pillararenes-based sensor [12], pyrene derivative fluorescent probe [13] and cerium-based metal-organic tetrahedron sensor [14] have been exploited as a fluorescent biosensors for tryptophan detection. However, their enormous aroma structure bring some inevitable drawbacks such as complicated synthesis procedures, poor water solubility, high toxicity and easy photobleaching, which primarily restricted their further applications, particularly in biological fields. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a simple, facile, low toxicity and high water soluble analytical approach for tryptophan detection in various aqueous solution especially in biological systems, which is significant to understand the pathogeny for clinical diagnosis and therapy.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a newest generation of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have been widely employed in bioimaging [15], drug delivery [16], light emitting diode [17], fluorescence sensors [18] and so on. All these CDs illustrated simple preparation process, low toxicity, high water solubility, good biocompatibility and excellent optical properties [19–21]. CDs as chemical and biological sensors have been applied extensively in chemical, environmental, and biological field, for instance heavy metal ions [22], pH [23], free radicals [24], and so on. However, there is only limited sensor based on CDs utilized for amino acids detection in biological samples, especially for tryptophan. Therefore, it is imperative and encouraging to develop a simple, facile, low toxicity and high-efficiency analytical approach for tryptophan detection in various conditions especially in biological systems.

In this report, we developed a novel analytical platform based on a pyridine functionalized carbon dots CDs (Py-CDs) for tryptophan detection, which was fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal route utilized pyridine (Py) derivative 3-aminoisonicotinic acid as the carbon and nitrogen sources, simultaneously. The as-prepared Py-CDs exhibited excellent water solubility, good photostability, high biocompatibility, and cellular imaging capability. More importantly, the surface of Py-CDs retained the relevant functional structures (pyridine ring) from the precursor 3-aminoisonicotinic acid, which served as an active site for tryptophan detection. The fluorescence of Py-CDs was quenched by the nonfluorescence composite formed by tryptophan and the pyridine ring on the as-prepared carbon dots. The biosensor illustrated high sensitivity and selectivity toward tryptophan with a detection limit of 5.7 nM, which was more sensitive than the conventional molecules sensor. In addition, the biosensor has realized tryptophan detection in human serum samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery, which manifested that the novel biosensor would be a promising analytical platform for monitoring tryptophan in biological fluid. Moreover, the biosensor is also successfully applied in intracellular imaging for tryptophan detection. These results demonstrated its bright prospect and great potential in practical application of tryptophan associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.

All solvents and chemicals utilized in this work were of analytical reagent grade and without further purification. 3-Aminoisonicotinic acid was purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3 and NaNO3 (≥ 99%) were all obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Arginine (Arg), asparticacid (Asp), alanine (Ala), cysteine (Cys), glutamine (Glu), histidine (His), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tyrosine (Tyr), glutathione (GSH), glucose and 1-methyl-d-tryptophan (Me-Trp) were provided by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Milli-Q-purified water was applied for sample preparations. The human serum samples were provided by Xi'an No. 1 Hospital (Shanxi, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of Py-CDs were obtained by Tecnai G2F20 microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded by a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey data were obtained by a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-4100 spectrophotometer. Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer was utilized to fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. The fluorescent lifetime was measured by Edinburgh FLS920. The cellular imaging was carried out using Nikon Ti2-E confocal laser fluorescence microscopy. Zeta potentials of the Py-CDs aqueous solution in the absence and presence of Trp were measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK).

The Py-CDs were synthesized by typical one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.138 g (1 mmol) 3-aminoisonicotinic acid was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water, which was put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180°C for 8 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally and the yellow reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove insoluble solids. Subsequently, the obtained supernatant was filtered by a 0.22 μm cellulose microporous membrane to remove larger particles. To further purificate the Py-CDs, the filtrate was dialyzed against continuously running deionized water through a dialysis membran (molecular weight cutoff 1000) for 24 h. Finally, the purified Py-CDs as a yellow powder was obtained after vacuum freeze drying for 24 h, which was collected and stored at 4°C for further application.

Firstly, 300 μL purified Py-CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) was put into 2.5 mL Tris–HCl buffer solution (pH=7.2). Then, different concentrations of tryptophan were separately added into the above solution. The mixture was diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water and incubated at room temperature for 2 min, the emission spectra were collected and analyzed on a fluorescence spectrophotometer. With the same method, the selectivity experiments of Py-CDs toward tryptophan was conducted by adding other biological substances solutions instead of tryptophan, including other amino acids, glucose, glutathione, metal ions or anions solutions. Based on the selectivity experiments, the competition experiments were performed by adding tryptophan to the Py-CD solutions with different interfering anions simultaneously. Complex real samples were utilized to evaluate the feasibility and applicability of the proposed analytical methodology. The human serum sample was removed large molecules and proteins by using a 3 kDa ultrafiltration tube. Then the treated human serum samples was diluted 10-fold before analysis, the following operations were the same as those for samples. The obtained samples were spiked with a series of concentrations of standard tryptophan solution.

L929 cells were cultured in the DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum at 37°C and antibiotics (100 g/mL streptomycin and 100 units/mL penicillin) in an incubator with an atmosphere containing 5% CO2 and 100% humidity. Five thousand L929 cells were prepared to evaluate cytotoxicity of Py-CDs in 96-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well that were incubated in 100 μL). After cell attachment for 12 h, different concentrations of Py-CDs (0–200 μg/mL) were added into the 96-well plate. After 24 h incubation, 10 μL 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution was added into each well. After 4 h incubation at 37°C, the medium was carefully removed and 150 μL DMSO was added to dissolve the purple formazan crystals. The plate was shaken for 10 min and the optical density at 490 nm was taken by a microplate reader. Each experiment was performed at least 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging and detection of tryptophan
ce:section.ce:para: L929 cells were grown overnight on a confocal microscope dish with a cover slip and 20 μg/mL Py-CDs was added to cells for 4 h. As a control group, the excess medium was removed and each dish was washed twice with 1.0 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). Meanwhile, the aboved treated cells were sequentially incubated with 20 and 40 μM tryptophan for 30 min at 37°C. Following that, cell imaging was observed with confocal fluorescence microscopy. Py-CDs was excited at 285 nm and the fluorescence was collected at 340 – 520 nm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were used to characterize the morphology and size of Py-CDs (Fig. 1 a,b), the nanoparticles monodispersed well and displayed smooth surface morphologies, and the uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter illustrated approximately 2.74 nm. Their size distribution agreed well with Image J software (80 Py-CDs measured), and the size distribution was 2.04 – 3.56 nm (Fig. 1c). In more detail, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) disclosed that Py-CDs possessed crystal structures with interlayer lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite carbon [25].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were employed to investigate surface functional groups of Py-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the Py-CDs possessed varieties of absorption bands of O − H stretching vibrations (2980 and 2905 cm −1), C = O stretching vibrations (1690 cm −1), C − O stretching vibrations (1415 cm −1), O − H stretching vibrations (1250 cm −1), which demonstrated the existence of carboxyl on the surface of Py-CDs. The characteristic vibration bands at 3405 and 3276 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of N − H. The peak at 1618 and 1575 cm −1 were associated with the C = C or C = N stretching vibration, which indicated the formation of pyridine ring within the carbon cores. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of Py-CDs didn't illustrate huge and significant difference contrast with the only precursor (3-aminoisonicotinic acid), which confirmed that carboxyl, amino and pyridine ring groups retained in the framework of the Py-CDs.

In order to futher identify the functional groups and elemental compositions of Py-CDs, the as-prepared Py-CDs were also characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full-range XPS spectrum of NCDs was shown in Fig. 2 a, the three obvious peaks located at 284.4, 398.8 and 532 eV were attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b) could be divided into four components at 284.5, 285.6, 287.1 and 288.2 eV, which associated with C − C/C = C, C − N, C = N bands and COOH group [26]. The N 1 s band (Fig. 2c) could be deconvoluted into three dominant peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (398.8 eV) amino N (399.5 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.6 eV) [27]. The high-resolved O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibited the distinctive peaks of C − O and C = O at 531.0 and 532.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d) [28]. Based on the XPS analysis, it is reconfirmed that the polar functional groups present on the surface of Py-CDs are carboxyl, amino and pyridine ring groups, which was well consistent with the results of FTIR.

In the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 a), Py-CDs showed relatively broad absorption range centered at around 310 nm, which resulted by the long conjugated aromatic π system and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [25]. As exhibited in Fig. 3b, the maximum emission wavelength located at 420 nm was observed while excited at 285 nm. Moreover, the Py-CDs illustrated an excitation-independent feature, the emission wavelength kept at 420 nm when the excitation wavelengths increased from 265 nm to 310 nm, which implied that a nearly uniform emission state exists in the Py-CDs surface (Fig. 3b) [29].

To optimize the response time for fluorescence quenching by tryptophan, the time-dependent experiment of the Py-CDs was recorded (Fig. 3c), which illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of Py-CDs rapidly quenched in 15 s and no apparent change in emission wavelength, implying the interaction between Py-CDs and tryptophan reaches an equilibrium state under a high sensitivity and extremely quick response. Compared with those organic molecules fluorescent sensor [30], the response time is much shorter and quicker, which attributed to the fast formation rate between tryptophan and the pyridine ring. The quick response rate was suitable and favorable for real-time dynamic monitoring Trp, which demonstrated Py-CDs was a potentially excellent nanoprobe for real-time detection in the different analytical field.

Photobleaching experiment was performed to examine the photostability of Py-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Py-CDs solution was continuously irradiated for 60 min at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, more than 92% of the fluorescence intensity of Py-CDs remained compared to its original fluorescence. Besides, the photostability experiment indicated that the fluorescence intensity of Py-CDs still remained steady even after one month at room temperature (Figure S1). Such outstanding photostability attributed to the surface structure and composition of Py-CDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of Py-CDs decreased drastically in a strong acidic solution but still possessed great fluorescence performance on in neutral and alkaline environment (Figure S2), which demonstrated Py-CDs can be utilized in biological system and in vivo analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Trp based on the Py-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the sensing performance of the Py-CDs toward Trp was studied. The introduction of Trp brough significant fluorescence quenching of Py-CDs, the observed fluorescence quenching by Trp could be ascribed to the complexation reaction between Trp and Py-CDs to form a stable composite, facilitating charge transfer from the excited state of the Py-CDs to Trp. The detailed sensing performances was shown in Fig.4 a. The fluorescence titrations of the Py-CDs with different concentrations of Trp (from 0 − 40 μM) showed a gradual quenching process. Moreover, excellent linear correlation of R2 = 0.997 between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and concentration of Trp were observed in the range of 0.02 − 20 μM (Fig.4b), which could be fitted into (F0/F) = 1.023 + 7.718 × 103 [Trp], corresponding to the Stern−Volmer equation: (F0/F) =1 + KSV [Trp], where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of the Py-CDs at 420 nm in the absence and presence of Trp, respectively; and KSV is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated from this plot is 5.7 nM according to the 3σ (signal-to-noise) criteria.

Moreover, to evaluate the selectivity of this sensing platform for Trp, interference of common biological substances coexisting in serum samples, including various amino acids (Ala, Arg, Asp, Cys, Gln, His, Ser, Thr, Tyr), inorganic ions (Na +, Ca 2+, Mg 2+, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 − and NO3 −), glutataione (GSH) and glucose, among others, were examined. The interfering experiments were explored with a variety of interfering substances (black bars, Fig. 4c,d), which clearly showed that the fluorescence intensity almost remains unchanged (< 8% decrease) except for Trp (> 95% decrease). This is attributed to the absence of specific interaction between surface functional groups of Py-CDs and biological substance. And the red bars shown in Fig. 4c,d showed the antijamming capability by subsequently adding 40 μM Trp to each solution. That implied the most biological substances can coexist in the solution for the Trp detection without any interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the optical response of Py-CDs to Trp
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of Py-CDs by Trp could be classified as static quenching, dynamic quenching mechanism or both [31,32]. To explore the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime decays of Py-CDs before and after the addition of Trp were employed to distinguish the quenching type of this process (Fig. 5 a). From the fluorescence emission decay curves analysis, the average lifetime time of Py-CDs (τ0) was found to be 7.69 ns. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime was unchanged after the addition of Trp, which implied a static quenching process occurring between Py-CDs and Trp. Besides, diffusion-controlled quenching constant (kq) was calculated by KSV = kqτ0, where τ0 = 7.69 ns. The obtained kq (1.094 × 1012 L mol−1 s − 1) was larger than the typical quenching constant for organic molecules sensor (1.0 − 2.0) × 1010 L•mol−1 s − 1, which once again suggested the quenching occurred via a static mechanism [33].

As we all known, the pyridine ring could be easy to combine with hydrogen protons. Notably, tryptophan could serve as a donor of hydrogen, which is attributed to the position N1 hydrogen of Trp. In addition, the surface of Py-CDs may have retained the active structure of pyridine ring during the synthesis process, which was demonstrated by the same absorption peaks with 3-aminoisonicotinic acid in FTIR spectrum of Py-CDs. Therefore, the surface active groups provided the possibility for Trp to interact with Py-CDs and form a Py-CDs-Trp complex. To prove this, 1-methyl-d-tryptophan (Me-Trp) was introduced to the Py-CDs analytical system to instead of Trp. However, there was no fluorescence quenching (Figure S3), indicating no complex between Me-Trp and Py-CDs, because the position N1 methy of Me-Trp didn't serve as a hydrogen donor. In addition, the zeta potential value changed from −14.2 to −4.07 mV after the addition of Trp (Figure S4a,b), which was attributed to the formation of stable composite between Trp and Py-CDs. The composite shielded the negative charge of the pyridine ring on the surface of Py-CDs and made the zeta potential more positive. Hence, all evidence supported the efficacy of Py-CDs as a fluorescence sensor for detecting Trp. When Trp were introduced to the Py-CDs analytical system, a strong interaction occurs between the N1 position hydrogen of Trp and the active structure of pyridine ring or other surface groups on the Py-CDs surface, which induced the formation of the Py-CDs-Trp complex and disrupts the surface state of Py-CDs, causing the quenching phenomenon of Py-CDs. Further mechanisms will be explored in future works. Scheme 1 described a possible sensing tactic toward Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Trp in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the potential practical applications of the Py-CDs analytical system, the nanoprobe was utilized to detect Trp in human serum. The serum sample was added in the Py-CDs system and the fluorescence intensity change was measured. As shown in Table 1 , high recovery rate and analytical precision were obtained. The satisfactory recovery rate of Trp in the Py-CDs analytical system were 96.1 − 105.6%, with RSDs of 1.3 − 3.2%. The result further confirmed the reliability and feasibility of the Py-CDs-based nanosensors for monitoring Trp. Therefore, this proposed fluorescent assay has potential for broaden Trp detection in actual human serum samples.

The performance of this biosensor of Trp was evaluated by comparison with other reported Fluorescent sensors. As shown in Table 2 , our work shows a rarely reported fluorescence CDs toward Trp detection with the high water solubility and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxcity test and imaging of Trp in living cell systems
ce:section.ce:para: As Trp commonly distributes in a variety of intercellular environments and plays important role in biological system. L929 cells were chosen as a model in vivo system for further appraising the biomedical applications of in vivo imaging. Firstly, cytotoxicity of Py-CDs to L929 cells was assessed by MTT standard assay. The cytotoxicity results shown in Fig. 5b indicated that Py-CDs possesses very low toxicity to normal cells because totacell viability of tested cells remains at the high level (> 91%) even when the concentration of Py-CD conjugate is up to 200 μg/mL, which manifested the promise of their exceptional potential for intracellular imaging in living cell systems.

The confocal fluorescence images of L929 cells were acquired after incubation by 20 μg/mL Py-CDs for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 6 a-c, the cells showed bright blue fluorescence signals. It could be concluded that Py-CDs demonstrate good cell permeability and efficiently accumulate in the cell membrane, cytoplasm not inside the cell nucleus. With further incubation of the cells with Trp, the intracellular fluorescence signal intensity was gradually reduced with an increase of the concentration of Trp, which proving that the process of fluorescence change in vivo was consistent with that in vitro. These positive results illustrated that Py-CDs as a sensing platform could be potentially used to real-time monitor Trp level in vivo and assist the treatment of tryptophan-associated diseases.

A rare example of CDs that detect tryptophan is reported, we design a novel biosensor based on CDs for effective and fast detection for Trp, which can be readily prepared via a one-step hydrothermal route utilized 3-aminoisonicotinic acid as nitrogen and carbon source, simultaneously. Without any extra surface modification, the as-prepared Py-CDs exhibited excellent analytical capability for Trp detection in biological samples and intracellular condition. In the presence of Trp, the fluorescence of Py-CDs can be distinctly decreased by static quenching because of the specific interaction between Trp and the active structures (Pyridine ring) on the surface of Py-CDs. More significantly, this method also has good linearity (0.02–20 µM) and low detection limit of 5.7 nM. Meanwhile, the established nanosensor also exhibited good biocompatibility, outstanding water solubility, high selectivity, low cytotoxicity and great cellular imaging capability, which could be successfully applied for screening Trp in human serum samples and imaging intracellular Trp, which demonstrated this biosensor has a great potential for widespread application for Trp-associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.

Wenshuai Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Saipeng Huang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Huiyun Wen: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yane Luo: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Jiewei Cheng: Resources, Validation. Zhao Jia: Resources. Pu Han: Resources. Weiming Xue: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project, China (2018JM2037) and Opening Project of the Key Laboratory of the Education Ministry of China (ZSK2018008). This study was also supported by Shaanxi Provincial Natural Science Basic Research Project (Youth) “2019JQ-461”.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104579.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.E. Dalgliesh, W.E. Knox, A. Neuberger, Intermediary metabolism of tryptophan, Nature168(1951) 20–22. 10.1038/168020a0

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of tryptophan using a glassy carbon electrode modified with flower-like structured nanocomposite consisting of reduced graphene oxide and SnO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Haldorai, S.H. Yeon, S.H. Yun, Y.K. Han, Electrochemical determination of tryptophan using a glassy carbon electrode modified with flower-like structured nanocomposite consisting of reduced graphene oxide and SnO2, Sens.
